summary: In this abstract of her 2009 keynote, Martha Nussbaum critiques the prevailing utilitarian approach to education that prioritizes marketable skills and economic profitability. She advocates for a humanistic approach centered on critical thinking and imagination, arguing these are essential for a healthy democracy, a decent world culture, and true human development.

principles.statement: Education must cultivate humanistic abilities like critical thinking and imagination, not just technical skills.
principles.rationale: These abilities are crucial for the health of democracy, the creation of a decent world culture, and a robust type of global citizenship.
principles.source_quote: It neglects the humanistic abilities of critical thinking and imagining that are so crucial if education is really to promote human development, rather than merely economic growth and individual acquisition.

principles.statement: Development should be understood as inclusive 'human development' rather than narrowly defined economic progress.
principles.rationale: A strictly economic conception of development focuses on profit and individual acquisition, which endangers the values necessary for the future of democracy.
principles.source_quote: ...between two conceptions of development: (1) the old narrowly economic conception of development; and (2) the richer more inclusive notion of "human development."

mental_models.name: Education for Profit vs. Education for Freedom
mental_models.description: A dichotomy used to evaluate educational systems. One model focuses on utilitarian, technical training to secure global market share and economic growth. The other focuses on cultivating humanistic abilities for democratic health and inclusive global citizenship.
mental_models.example: Evaluating current educational trends that rush toward profitability and technical training while neglecting the arts and humanities.

signature_quotes.text: With the rush to profitability, values precious for the future of democracy are in danger of getting lost.
signature_quotes.context: Warning against the utilitarian shift in educational priorities.

signature_quotes.text: It neglects the humanistic abilities of critical thinking and imagining that are so crucial if education is really to promote human development, rather than merely economic growth and individual acquisition.
signature_quotes.context: Critiquing even the best practitioners of the human development approach for focusing too heavily on marketable skills.

anti_patterns.description: Discussing and designing education solely in low-level utilitarian terms to produce technically trained workers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It focuses entirely on global market share and profitability, putting the abilities crucial to the health of democracy and global citizenship at risk of getting lost in the competitive flurry.

anti_patterns.description: Practitioners of the 'human development' approach focusing primarily on basic marketable skills.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It neglects the critical thinking and imagination required to promote actual human development, inadvertently defaulting back to a focus on mere economic growth.
